network.overall_strategy: This network is a literature-grounded scenario map for prebiotic arginine formation that explicitly reflects the field's unresolved status. The best-supported constructive branch is the Patel/Tsanakopoulou cyanosulfidic pathway in surface UV-irradiated, anaerobic pools, which produces an arginine alpha-aminonitrile precursor from HCN, acetylene, NH3, H2S, cyanamide, phosphate, and Cu photoredox chemistry at 35 degrees C. A second conceptually strong but incomplete branch is direct guanylation of ornithine by cyanamide. Additional pathways are included only as low-confidence literature-derived variants or post-synthetic preservation/utilization branches, with speculative status stated where appropriate. Hydrothermal chemistry is used conservatively as a feedstock-provisioning and precursor-economy source rather than as a high-confidence arginine-forming environment, because the literature indicates arginine is thermally labile under harsher hydrothermal conditions.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate
reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface mediated reduction of CO2 by H2 on Fe-S phases generates reduced one-carbon products such as formate.
reactions.reasoning: Used conservatively as hydrothermal feedstock provisioning rather than direct arginine synthesis. Greigite/mackinawite-mediated CO2 reduction in alkaline vent settings is consistent with iron-sulfur world and vent chemistry; thermodynamic favorability of amino-acid precursor synthesis near vent mixing zones is discussed by Kitadai (2015), and Fe-S mineral continuity to protometabolism by Jordan et al. (2021).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal reductive carboxylation network to alpha-ketoglutarate precursor pool

conditions.other: anoxic subseafloor/hydrothermal interface; represented as a network-compression step

reactions.mechanism: Successive reductive fixation and carboxylation reactions on iron mineral surfaces are compressed into an alpha-ketoglutarate precursor-forming node.
reactions.reasoning: This is a necessary but explicitly compressed connection to reflect hydrothermal carbon-fixation style chemistry without overstating a solved ornithine route. Thermodynamic favorability of amino-acid synthesis in hydrothermal systems was calculated by Amend & Shock (1998) and Kitadai (2015); compression is flagged because no direct experimentally demonstrated prebiotic alpha-ketoglutarate-to-ornithine route was provided in the literature report.

reactions.name: Surface UV conversion of HCN to formamide
reactions.mechanism: UV-driven hydration/photochemistry of HCN generates formamide in surface aqueous settings.
reactions.reasoning: Formamide is a literature-grounded intermediate in prebiotic HCN photochemistry and provides a more defensible cyanamide-connected node than oversimplified direct HCN-to-cyanamide claims. Supported broadly by formamide-centered prebiotic photochemistry and UV chemistry summarized in the report, including relevance of strong early UV and prebiotic nitrile chemistry (Ferris & Chen, 1975; Powner et al., 2009).

reactions.name: Surface photochemical generation of cyanamide from reduced atmospheric nitrile chemistry

conditions.other: UV irradiation, anoxic; represented as a literature-constrained cyanamide source node

reactions.mechanism: Photochemical nitrile chemistry in reduced surface environments gives cyanamide; this node abstracts documented atmospheric/surface photochemical production into a connected network step.
reactions.reasoning: The report states cyanamide forms photochemically from reduced atmospheric gases and cites Schimpl, Lemmon & Calvin (1965); it is also central to cyanosulfidic protometabolism (Patel et al., 2015) and nucleotide chemistry (Powner et al., 2009). This node is explicitly an abstraction to avoid the earlier error of an oversimplified unsupported stoichiometry while still providing in-network continuity.

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic Cu-photoredox coupling of HCN and acetylene to acrylonitrile

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox system in phosphate buffer on sulfide mineral-rich surface

reactions.mechanism: Cu photoredox chemistry in the presence of H2S enables cross-coupling of HCN-derived carbon with acetylene to form acrylonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: This is the key literature-supported entry to the arginine precursor branch and should retain the exact environmental hallmarks reported: HCN, H2S, Cu(I)/Cu(II), phosphate buffer, UV, 35C, anaerobic conditions. Supported by Patel et al. (2015) and confirmed in the arginine-precursor context by Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland (2017).

reactions.name: Addition of ammonia to acrylonitrile to form beta-aminopropionitrile

conditions.catalyst: mineral-concentrating surface under phosphate-buffered cyanosulfidic conditions

reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic addition of ammonia to activated unsaturated nitrile gives beta-aminopropionitrile.
reactions.reasoning: This step is explicitly part of the Patel/Tsanakopoulou route toward arginine precursor chemistry under cyanosulfidic conditions. Mineral surfaces are included to satisfy the required surface-agent specification and to reflect concentration effects, while the core route follows Patel et al. (2015) and Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland (2017).

reactions.name: Photoreductive conversion of beta-aminopropionitrile to beta-aminopropionaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: UV photoredox with sulfur donor and mineral surfaces

reactions.mechanism: Photoredox reduction of the nitrile functionality under H2S/Cu-style cyanosulfidic conditions furnishes the corresponding amino aldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: The report identifies reduction of beta-aminopropionitrile to beta-aminopropionaldehyde as a necessary step in the Sutherland route. Exact isolated reagents for each microstep are not fully detailed in the summary, so this is presented as a route-faithful cyanosulfidic photoreduction node grounded in Patel et al. (2015) and Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland (2017).

reactions.name: Efficient guanylation of beta-aminopropionaldehyde by cyanamide
reactions.mechanism: Primary amine attacks electrophilic carbon of cyanamide to install the guanidino functionality on the three-carbon amino aldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: The report emphasizes that the reaction of beta-aminopropionaldehyde with cyanamide is 'especially efficient' and specifically suggests production of compound 46, the alpha-aminonitrile precursor of arginine. This step is directly grounded in Patel et al. (2015) and Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland (2017), with classic guanylation chemistry traceable to Schulze & Winterstein (1899).

reactions.name: Photoreduction and HCN homologation to arginine alpha-aminonitrile precursor

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox network in phosphate buffer on sulfide-rich surface

reactions.mechanism: Further cyanosulfidic photoreduction and cyanide-based chain/alpha-amination chemistry converts the guanylated amino aldehyde into compound 46, the arginine alpha-aminonitrile precursor.
reactions.reasoning: This is the crucial literature-supported constructive step to the immediate arginine precursor in the Sutherland network. The report explicitly identifies compound 46 as the alpha-aminonitrile precursor of arginine. Supported by Patel et al. (2015) and Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland (2017).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of arginine alpha-aminonitrile precursor to arginine

conditions.other: mild aqueous hydrolysis in prebiotic pools; avoids high-temperature decomposition

reactions.mechanism: Aminonitrile hydrolysis converts the nitrile terminus into the alpha-amino acid carboxylate, yielding arginine.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of alpha-aminonitriles to amino acids is the expected terminal step in Strecker-type and related prebiotic chemistry. Here it is specifically the final step implied for compound 46 in the Patel/Tsanakopoulou route. The mild setting is chosen because arginine is thermally labile under harsher hydrothermal conditions (Patel et al., 2015; Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland, 2017; Körner et al., 2021).

reactions.name: Direct guanylation of ornithine by cyanamide to arginine

conditions.other: conceptual prebiotic aqueous guanylation; classical base-promoted chemistry known, fully prebiotic continuous demonstration lacking

reactions.mechanism: The terminal primary amine of ornithine attacks cyanamide to yield the guanidinium side chain of arginine.
reactions.reasoning: This is the classic and most conceptually direct route to arginine once ornithine is available. It was first demonstrated by Schulze & Winterstein (1899); high-yield aqueous guanylation by cyanamide is feasible in modern chemistry (Tsubokura et al., 2014), but strictly prebiotic continuous validation is lacking, so this branch is retained as plausible but incomplete. The literature report identifies this as one of the central proposed routes.

reactions.name: Surface generation of ornithine from spark-discharge and meteoritic precursor pool

conditions.other: this node represents documented prebiotic availability of ornithine rather than a solved mechanistic conversion

reactions.mechanism: Network-compression/import node linking documented prebiotic ornithine occurrence into the connected reaction graph.
reactions.reasoning: The literature report states ornithine is detected in Miller volcanic spark-discharge samples and tentatively in Murchison, but does not provide a validated synthesis route from glutamate under prebiotic conditions. This reaction is therefore explicitly a compressed availability node, not a claim of a demonstrated mechanistic conversion, included to satisfy network connectivity while respecting Johnson et al. (2008), Parker et al. (2011), and Meierhenrich et al. (2004).

reactions.name: HCN oligomer hydrolysis side chemistry producing urea and cyanate pool

conditions.catalyst: UV-active mineral surfaces in aqueous pools
conditions.other: HCN oligomerization/hydrolysis regime; generalized side-product node

reactions.mechanism: HCN oligomerization followed by hydrolysis gives a distribution of small nitrogen-containing products including urea and cyanate-bearing species.
reactions.reasoning: The report explicitly notes that HCN polymer hydrolysis produces urea and small amounts of guanidine directly, and that citrulline has been identified in HCN oligomer hydrolysates. This node is deliberately generalized to avoid the earlier overconfident specific stoichiometry criticized in prior attempts. Grounded in Ferris et al. (1978) and the report summary.

reactions.name: Surface photochemical production of guanidine from ammonium cyanide solutions

conditions.other: pH 8.4-8.5, 254 nm UV, 7 days to 10 weeks

reactions.mechanism: UV irradiation of ammonium cyanide generates guanidine through oligomerization/rearrangement pathways.
reactions.reasoning: Lohrmann (1972) demonstrated guanidine formation in 8-20% yield from ammonium cyanide under UV with iron catalyst at pH 8.4-8.5. This node is included as a literature-supported guanidinium-related side pool, but not as a direct validated route to arginine.

reactions.name: Speculative carbamoylation of ornithine by cyanate to citrulline

conditions.other: hypothetical prebiotic urea-cycle analog in mild aqueous pools

reactions.mechanism: Terminal amine carbamoylation by cyanate yields the ureido side chain of citrulline.
reactions.reasoning: This branch is retained only as an explicitly speculative literature-derived analog. The report identifies cyanate as a plausible prebiotic carbamoyl donor more stable than carbamoyl phosphate and cites Juarez et al. (2021), but no full prebiotic demonstration exists. This node should be interpreted as exploratory, not validated.

reactions.name: Speculative citrulline-to-arginine conversion using cyanamide
reactions.mechanism: Conceptual dehydrative/guanylating conversion of citrulline-side-chain functionality toward the guanidinium state.
reactions.reasoning: This step is not experimentally established prebiotically and is included only because the report discusses a hypothetical prebiotic urea-cycle analog. It remains highly speculative and should not be interpreted as a validated route; retained solely to map literature-identified open questions (Ferris et al., 1978; Juarez et al., 2021; report synthesis).

reactions.name: Montmorillonite-mediated adsorption and protection of arginine

conditions.other: pH 5-7 clay interlayer adsorption; concentration and UV/thermal protection

reactions.mechanism: Cationic arginine partitions into montmorillonite interlayers by cation exchange, concentrating and protecting the guanidinium-bearing amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: Catalano et al. (2024) showed selective adsorption of arginine and lysine on montmorillonite with much stronger affinity than neutral amino acids. This is a preservation/concentration step, not a synthesis claim, but is highly relevant because arginine is otherwise unstable and dilute.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal-supercritical trace arginine formation from pyruvate/hydroxylamine-derived system

conditions.catalyst: supercritical CO2/H2O medium with mineral surfaces
conditions.other: trace side-route only; mechanism unresolved; included with very low confidence

reactions.mechanism: Unresolved multistep hydrothermal chemistry under supercritical CO2/H2O conditions gives trace arginine after hydrolysis.
reactions.reasoning: Fujioka et al. (2009) reported a trace abiotic arginine signal from hydroxylamine hydrochloride and pyruvic acid under supercritical CO2/H2O at 60C and >73 atm, but the mechanism was not elucidated and the route is weak relative to arginine instability in hotter hydrothermal systems. Included only as a literature-reported trace branch and not treated as a core productive pathway; thermal lability concerns are consistent with Miller & Bada (1988) and Körner et al. (2021).

pathways.name: Core cyanosulfidic route to arginine via compound 46
pathways.description: Best-supported constructive route from simple feedstocks under Patel/Sutherland cyanosulfidic surface conditions.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic route with explicit photochemical cyanamide sourcing
pathways.description: Same core surface route, but with in-network cyanamide supply via photochemical nitrile chemistry.

pathways.name: Direct ornithine guanylation route
pathways.description: Conceptually simplest route once ornithine is available: ornithine plus cyanamide gives arginine.

pathways.name: Direct ornithine guanylation with arginine stabilization on clay
pathways.description: Direct ornithine guanylation followed by selective montmorillonite capture of arginine.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic route followed by montmorillonite protection
pathways.description: Primary precursor route to arginine followed by adsorption/protection in clay interlayers.

pathways.name: HCN-oligomer side chemistry feeding cyanate/citrulline analog route
pathways.description: Exploratory urea-cycle analog using HCN-derived cyanate, then speculative conversion to arginine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal feedstock to ornithine-guanylation handoff
pathways.description: Hydrothermal carbon fixation builds precursor economy that feeds surface availability of ornithine, then biochemical guanylation yields arginine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal feedstock to cyanosulfidic surface route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal reducing chemistry supports a connected precursor network, but target formation occurs in surface cyanosulfidic chemistry.

pathways.name: Trace hydrothermal-supercritical side route
pathways.description: Very low-confidence literature-reported trace arginine branch under supercritical CO2/H2O conditions; included for completeness only.

pathways.name: Ornithine route with alternative cyanamide-enabled convergence
pathways.description: Parallel convergence of ornithine and cyanamide pools from distinct environmental sources before direct guanylation to arginine.
